name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يَشْتَرُونَ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةَ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَن تَضِلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who purchase error and desire that you [too] should lose the way? (44)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدَآئِكُمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ نَصِيرًا (٤٥)
```

```english
But Allah knows your enemies better, and Allah suffices as guardian, and Allah suffices as helper. (45)
```

```arabic
مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ وَرَٰعِنَا لَيًّۢا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْنًا فِى ٱلدِّينِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا وَٱسْمَعْ وَٱنظُرْنَا لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ وَأَقْوَمَ وَلَـٰكِن لَّعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٤٦)
```

```english
Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Hear without listening!’ and ‘Ra’ina,’ twisting their tongues and reviling the faith. But had they said, ‘We hear and obey’ and ‘Listen’ and ‘Unzurna,’ it would have been better for them and more upright. But Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness, so they will not believe except a few. (46)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِهَآ أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ كَمَا لَعَنَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّبْتِ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ ٱللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا (٤٧)
```

```english
O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards, or curse them as We cursed the People of the Sabbath, and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled. (47)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ وَيَغْفِرُ مَا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدِ ٱفْتَرَىٰٓ إِثْمًا عَظِيمًا (٤٨)
```

```english
Indeed Allah does not forgive that partners should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. Whoever ascribes partners to Allah has indeed fabricated [a lie] in great sinfulness. (48)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنفُسَهُم ۚ بَلِ ٱللَّهُ يُزَكِّى مَن يَشَآءُ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٤٩)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who style themselves as pure? Indeed, it is Allah who purifies whomever He wishes, and they will not be wronged [so much as] a single date-thread. (49)
```

```arabic
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦٓ إِثْمًا مُّبِينًا (٥٠)
```

```english
Look, how they fabricate lies against Allah! That suffices for a flagrant sin. (50)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْجِبْتِ وَٱلطَّـٰغُوتِ وَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَهْدَىٰ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ سَبِيلًا (٥١)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, believing in idols and fake deities and saying of the pagans: ‘These are better guided on the way than the faithful’? (51)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَمَن يَلْعَنِ ٱللَّهُ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ نَصِيرًا (٥٢)
```

```english
They are the ones whom Allah has cursed, and whomever Allah curses, you will never find any helper for him. (52)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُلْكِ فَإِذًا لَّا يُؤْتُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ نَقِيرًا (٥٣)
```

```english
Do they have a share in sovereignty? If so, they will not give those people [so much as] a speck on a date-stone! (53)
```

```arabic
أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَىٰ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۖ فَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ ءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مُّلْكًا عَظِيمًا (٥٤)
```

```english
Do they envy those people for what Allah has given them out of His bounty? We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom, and We have given them a great sovereignty. (54)
```

```arabic
فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن صَدَّ عَنْهُ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيرًا (٥٥)
```

```english
Of them are some who believe in him, and of them are some who deter [others] from him; and hell suffices for a blaze! (55)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ نَارًا كُلَّمَا نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُم بَدَّلْنَـٰهُمْ جُلُودًا غَيْرَهَا لِيَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا (٥٦)
```

```english
Indeed We shall soon make those who deny Our signs enter a Fire: as often as their skins become scorched, We shall replace them with other skins, so that they may taste the punishment. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (56)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَنُدْخِلُهُمْ ظِلًّا ظَلِيلًا (٥٧)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. In it, there will be chaste mates for them, and We shall admit them into a deep shade. (57)
```

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تُؤَدُّوا۟ ٱلْأَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا وَإِذَا حَكَمْتُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ أَن تَحْكُمُوا۟ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا (٥٨)
```

```english
Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their [rightful] owners, and to judge with fairness when you judge between people. Excellent indeed is what Allah advises you. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (58)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses address the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and detail their injustices and betrayals regarding the religion of Allah. They apply most clearly to the Jews and possess a single, continuous context. Regarding the final verse: "Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their right owners" (إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تُؤَدُّوا الْأَماناتِ إِلى أَهْلِها) [4:58], although some have mentioned it is Meccan and excepted it along with another verse from the Medinan Surah al-Nisa—namely this verse and His saying, the Exalted: "They ask you for a ruling. Say, ‘Allah gives you a ruling concerning the kalālah’" (يَسْتَفْتُونَكَ قُلِ اللهُ يُفْتِيكُمْ فِي الْكَلالَةِ) [4:176] according to what is in *al-Majma‘*—the verse is clearly linked to those preceding it. Similarly, the verse regarding the request for a ruling (الاستفتاء) concerns inheritance, which was legislated in Medina.

His saying, the Exalted: "Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book?" (أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ) [4:44]. It was previously mentioned in the discussion of verses (36–42) that they are somewhat linked to these verses, and you have heard the discourse regarding the revelation of those verses concerning the Jews.

In short, it appears from these verses that the Jews used to offer affection to the believers and display sincerity toward them, thereby tempting them. They would command them toward stinginess and withholding from spending in order to prevent their efforts from succeeding and to hinder their diligence in advancement and elevation. This is a necessary consequence of these verses being revealed concerning the Jews, or concerning those who used to associate with and befriend the Jews, then deviated from the truth due to their distortion, inclining toward wherever they inclined them, thus becoming stingy and then commanding stinginess.

This is what is gathered from His saying: "and desire that you should go astray from the way. But Allah knows your enemies better" (وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ تَضِلُّوا السَّبِيلَ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِأَعْدائِكُمْ) [4:44-45] to the end of the verse.

The meaning of the two verses—and Allah knows best—is that what We are clarifying to you confirms what We explained regarding the state of the one who withholds from spending in the way of Allah out of vanity, pride, stinginess, and hypocrisy. You see the Jews who were given a share (نصيباً) of the Book—meaning a portion of it, not all of it as they claim for themselves—purchasing error and choosing it over guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path*. They desire that you should stray from the path, for even if they meet you with a cheerful face, appear to you in the garb of righteousness, and connect with you as helping allies, mentioning to you what your temperaments might find pleasing and your hearts might deem correct, they desire nothing but your deviation from the path, just as they chose error for themselves. Allah knows your enemies better than you do, and they are your enemies. So let not the outward state you witness from them deceive you; beware of obeying their command or listening to their embellished words and decorative suggestions while you reckon they are your guardians and helpers. You have no need for their false guardianship or their hoped-for help; "Allah suffices as a guardian, and Allah suffices as a helper" (وَكَفى بِاللهِ وَلِيًّا وَكَفى بِاللهِ نَصِيراً) [4:45]. What need is there, alongside His guardianship and help, for their guardianship and help?

His saying, the Exalted: "Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ) [4:46] until His saying: "in religion" (فِي الدِّينِ). The word "among" (مِنَ) in His saying "Among those" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ) is explanatory (بيانية), serving as an explanation for His saying in the previous verse: "those who were given a share of the Book" (الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ), or for His saying: "your enemies" (بِأَعْدائِكُمْ). It has also been said that His saying "Among those who are Jews" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا) is a predicate for a suppressed subject, which is the described noun omitted for His saying "who pervert words" (يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ). The estimation would be: "Among those who are Jews is a people who pervert," or "Among those who are Jews are those who pervert." They said that the omission of the described noun is common, as in the saying of Dhu al-Rummah:

*So they remained, and among them was one whose tears preceded him,*
*And another who sheds the eye's tears with slow deliberation.*

He means: "And among them was a person whose tears..." or "And among them was one whose tears..." Allah the Exalted has described this group as perverting words from their meanings. This is either by changing the positions of the words through advancement, delay, omission, or addition—as is attributed to the existing Torah—or by interpreting what was narrated from Moses (عليه السلام) in the Torah and from other prophets in a way other than the intended true meaning. For instance, they misinterpreted the glad tidings in the Torah regarding the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), just as they previously misinterpreted the glad tidings regarding the Messiah (عليه السلام), saying that the promised one had not yet come; they await his arrival to this day.

It is possible that the intended meaning of perverting words from their meanings is what He, the Exalted, will mention in His saying: "and say, ‘We hear and disobey’" (وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنا وَعَصَيْنا). Thus, these phrases would be coordinated with His saying: "they pervert" (يُحَرِّفُونَ). The meaning of perverting words from their meanings would then be using a statement by placing it in a context other than where it ought to be placed. For the speaker's saying "We hear" (سمعنا), its right is to be placed in the context of obedience, such that it is said: "We hear and obey" (سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا), not that it is said: "We hear and disobey," or that "We hear" is placed in a context of sarcasm and mockery. Similarly, for the speaker's saying "Listen" (اسمع), it ought to be said: "Listen, may Allah make you hear," not that it is said: "Listen, without being heard" (اسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ)—meaning: may Allah not make you hear—and "Rā‘inā" (راعنا), which in the language of the Jews conveys the meaning of "Listen, without being heard."

His saying: "twisting their tongues and reviling the religion" (لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْناً فِي الدِّينِ). The root of *layy* (اللي) is twisting; that is, they incline with their tongues, presenting falsehood in their speech in the form of truth, and contempt and insult in the form of etiquette and respect. For the believers, when they spoke to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), used to address him by saying: "Rā‘inā, O Messenger of Allah," meaning: "Look at us and listen to us so that we may fulfill our purpose in our speech." The Jews seized upon this and would address the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: "Rā‘inā," intending by it a repulsive meaning they held, unbefitting of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) station. Thus, they were censured for it in this verse, which is His saying, the Exalted: "they pervert words from their meanings" (يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ), then He explained it with His saying: "and say, ‘We hear and disobey,’ and ‘Listen, without being heard’" (وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنا وَعَصَيْنا وَاسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ). Then He coordinated with it, as an explanatory coordination, His saying: "and ‘Rā‘inā’" (وَراعِنا). Then He mentioned that this blameworthy action of theirs is a twisting of tongues and a reviling of the religion, saying: "twisting their tongues and reviling the religion" (لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْناً فِي الدِّينِ). The two verbal nouns are in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال), and the estimation is: "twisting with their tongues and reviling the religion."

His saying, the Exalted: "Had they said, ‘We hear and obey,’ and ‘Listen,’ and ‘Unẓurnā,’ it would have been better for them and more upright" (وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ قالُوا سَمِعْنا وَأَطَعْنا لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَأَقْوَمَ) [4:46]. The fact that this statement from them—which contains the etiquette of religion and submission to the truth—is better and more upright than what they said (despite it containing blameworthy twisting and reviling, with no good or uprightness in it) is based on a comparison between the true effect within this true speech and what they imagine to be the effect in their own speech, even if it does not truly possess that. Thus, the comparison is between the true effect and the effect imagined to be true. The meaning is: had they said, "We hear and obey," there would have been more goodness and uprightness in it than what they reckon for themselves in this twisting and reviling. The speech follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: "When they sight some merchandise or diversion, they scatter toward it and leave you standing. Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and merchandise, and Allah is the best of providers’" (وَإِذا رَأَوْا تِجارَةً أَوْ لَهْواً انْفَضُّوا إِلَيْها وَتَرَكُوكَ قائِماً قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ) [62:11].

His saying, the Exalted: "But Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness, so they do not believe except a few" (وَلكِنْ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً) [4:46]. This is to make the listeners despair of the Jews saying "We hear and obey," for it is a word of faith (إيمان), and these are cursed ones who are not granted the success to believe. For this reason, it was said: "Had they said" (لو أنهم قالوا), which indicates a wish implying impossibility.

It appears that the "bi" (بـ) in His saying "for their faithlessness" (بِكُفْرِهِمْ) denotes causality (السببية) rather than the state itself, for faithlessness (الكفر) can be removed by faith. Thus, as faithlessness, it does not necessitate a curse that categorically prevents faith. However, when they disbelieved (and Allah the Exalted will explain the state of their faithlessness at the end of the Surah), Allah cursed them because of that with a curse that bound faithlessness upon them such that they do not believe except a few. Understand this.

As for His saying: "so they do not believe except a few" (فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً), it has been said that "few" (قَلِيلاً) is a circumstantial clause, and the estimation is: "except while they are few"—meaning they do not believe except in a state where they are few. It has also been said that "few" is an adjective for a suppressed noun, and the estimation is: "so they do not believe except a few beliefs." This view, like the previous one, is acceptable, but it must be added that the description of faith as "few" is of the type where the description applies to the state of what it relates to—meaning a faith whose objects of belief are few.

As for what some commentators mentioned—that the meaning is "little faith" in contrast to "complete faith," stating that the meaning is: "they do not believe except with a little faith that is not reckoned with, as it does not rectify the deeds of its possessor, nor purify his soul, nor elevate his intellect"—he has erred. For faith is described as stable or transient, complete or deficient in various degrees and ranks. As for "fewness" and its opposite "multitude," it is not described by them, especially in something like the Quran, which is the most eloquent of speech.

Furthermore, the faith mentioned in the verse is either the reality of heart-felt faith in contrast to hypocrisy (النفاق), or the outward form of faith which is sometimes called submission (الإسلام). Its consideration in any of its meanings and the attention paid to it in Islam is beyond doubt, and the Quranic verses are explicit regarding it. He, the Exalted, said: "and do not say to someone who offers you peace, ‘You are not a believer’" (وَلا تَقُولُوا لِمَنْ أَلْقى إِلَيْكُمُ السَّلامَ لَسْتَ مُؤْمِناً) [4:94]. Moreover, that which Allah the Exalted excludes from His saying: "But Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness," the lowest degrees of faith or outward Islam would have sufficed for it, by their maintaining outward appearances through saying "We hear and obey" like other Muslims.

What led him into this error was his illusion that His curse upon them for their faithlessness could not fail to take effect regarding the faith of some of them. Thus, he reckoned that "fewness" was a description of the faith—that which is not reckoned with—so that the saying "Allah has cursed them for their faithlessness" would remain consistent. He overlooked that these addresses, and the descriptions of censure, accountability, and rebuke they contain, are all directed toward societies qua societies. Thus, that which was struck by the curse, wrath, and other general accountabilities is the Jewish society insofar as it is a constituted society; thus they do not believe, nor find happiness, nor succeed. This remains so to this day, and they shall remain so until the Day of Resurrection.

As for the exception, it is only with respect to individuals. The exit of some individuals from a judgment decreed upon the society is not a violation of that judgment. What necessitated this exception is that individuals are, in a sense, the society. Thus, His saying: "so they do not believe," wherein faith is negated from the individuals—even if that is a negation from them from the perspective of the collective—it was possible to imagine that the judgment encompasses every single one of them such that no one escapes it. Thus, the exception was made: "except a few" (إِلَّا قَلِيلاً). The verse follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: "And had We decreed for them, ‘Kill yourselves,’ or ‘Leave your homes,’ they would not have done it except a few of them" (وَلَوْ أَنَّا كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ أَنِ اقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ أَوِ اخْرُجُوا مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ ما فَعَلُوهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ مِنْهُمْ) [4:66].

His saying, the Exalted: "O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down..." etc. **Effacement (الطمس)** is the erasing of the trace of a thing. The **face (الوجه)** is what confronts you of a thing and appears from it; in a human, it is the front, visible side of the head and what confronts you from it. It is used for conceptual matters just as it is used for sensory matters. **Backs (الأدبار)** is the plural of *dubur*, which is the nape of the neck. The "People of the Sabbath" (أصحاب السبت) refers to a group of Jews who transgressed on the Sabbath, so Allah cursed them and transformed them. He, the Exalted, said: "Ask them about the town that was by the seaside, when they violated the Sabbath—when their fish would come to them on their Sabbath day, visibly on the shore, but on days when they did not keep Sabbath they would not come to them" (وَسْئَلْهُمْ عَنِ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِي السَّبْتِ إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً وَيَوْمَ لا يَسْبِتُونَ لا تَأْتِيهِمْ) [7:163]. And He, the Exalted, said: "And certainly you know those of you who violated the Sabbath, whereupon We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes.’ So We made it an exemplary punishment for those who were then present and those who came after" (وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ الَّذِينَ اعْتَدَوْا مِنْكُمْ فِي السَّبْتِ فَقُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خاسِئِينَ فَجَعَلْناها نَكالاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْها وَما خَلْفَها) [2:65-66].

The previous verses—as you have known—addressed the state of the Jews or a group of them. The discourse led to the fact that in return for their betrayal of Allah and His Messenger and their corruption of the righteousness of their religion, they were afflicted with a curse from Allah that struck their collective and deprived them of the success to believe, except for a few. Thus, the address became general to all the People of the Book—as indicated by His saying: "O you who were given the Book" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ)—calling them to believe in the Book He sent down confirming what is with them, and warning them of the displeasure that would strike them if they rebelled and grew arrogant without excuse, consisting of effacement or a curse that would follow them inevitably.

That is what He mentioned in His saying: "before We efface faces and turn them into their backs" (مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَطْمِسَ وُجُوهاً فَنَرُدَّها عَلى أَدْبارِها) [4:47]. The effacement of faces is the erasing of these faces through which humans direct themselves toward their vital goals, which contain the expected and hoped-for happiness of man. However, it is not an erasure that necessitates the annihilation of the faces, their disappearance, or the nullification of their traces, but rather an erasure that necessitates the turning back of those faces onto their backs. Thus, they seek their goals according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — upon which they were created, but since they are set toward the napes and turned onto the backs, they seek nothing but what they have left behind them, and they do not walk toward it except backward.

This human—who by nature and primordial nature is directed toward what he sees as good and happiness for himself—whenever he directs himself toward what he sees as good for himself and righteousness for his religion or his world, he attains nothing but evil and corruption. Whenever he exerts himself in advancing, he increases in retreating, and he shall never succeed.

As for cursing them like the curse of the People of the Sabbath, its apparent meaning is transformation (المسخ), based on what preceded from the verses of the People of the Sabbath which inform of their transformation into apes.

Accordingly, the word "or" (أَوْ) in His saying "or curse them" (أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ) is according to its literal meaning of expressing an alternative. The difference between the two threats is that the first—meaning effacement—necessitates a change in the goals of those upon whom wrath has fallen without a change in physical form except in some of its qualities. The second—meaning the curse like the curse of the People of the Sabbath—necessitates a change in the goal by changing the human form into an animal form, such as apes.

These people, if they rebel against compliance—and they shall rebel, as indicated by the conclusion of the verse—will face one of two displeasures: either the effacement of faces or the curse like the curse of the People of the Sabbath. However, the verse indicates that this displeasure does not encompass all of them, as He said: "faces" (وُجُوهاً), using the indefinite plural. Had the intention been all of them, it would not have been indefinite. There is another subtle point in the indefiniteness of "faces" and the lack of their specification: since the context is one of threat and intimidation—a threat to the group with an evil that will only strike some of them—the ambiguity of the individuals upon whom the divine displeasure will fall is more effective in warning and frightening. This is because their description, in its ambiguity, is capable of applying to every single one of the people, so none of them feels safe from being touched by this miserable punishment. This technique is common in language in contexts of threat and intimidation.

In His saying, the Exalted: "or curse them" (أَوْ نَلْعَنَهُمْ), where He returned the pronoun "them" (هم)—designated for rational beings—to His saying "faces" (وُجُوهاً), as is apparent, there is an allusion or explicit statement that the intended meaning of "faces" is the persons from the perspective of their confronting their goals. By this, the possibility weakens that the intended meaning of effacing faces and turning them onto their backs is the physical turning of the faces of the bodies toward the napes, as some have said. This strengthens the possibility that the intended meaning of turning the faces to the backs is the turning of the souls from a state of uprightness of thought and the perception of realities as they are, to a state of deviation and intellectual degradation, such that they do not witness a truth except that they turn away from it and are disgusted by it, nor a falsehood except that they incline toward it and become obsessed with it.

This is a type of divine disposal out of loathing and vengeance, similar to what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "We shall turn their hearts and their sights, just as they did not believe in it the first time, and We shall leave them to flounder in their rebellion" (وَنُقَلِّبُ أَفْئِدَتَهُمْ وَأَبْصارَهُمْ كَما لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَنَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ) [6:110].

It has become clear from what has passed that the intended meaning of effacing faces in the verse is a type of divine disposal in the souls that necessitates changing their natures from following the truth and avoiding falsehood to following falsehood and shunning the truth regarding faith in Allah and His signs. This is supported by the beginning of the verse: "Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We efface..." etc. Likewise, it has become clear that the curse mentioned therein is transformation.

It has been said that the intended meaning of effacement is the physical turning of the faces of a people toward their napes, and that this will occur at the end of time or on the Day of Resurrection. To this it is replied: His saying "or curse them" contradicts that, as previously explained.

It has also been said that the intended meaning of effacement is worldly abandonment (الخذلان), such that they remain in humiliation and calamity, never seeking a goal of happiness except that Allah changes it for them into a mirage in which there is no good. To this it is replied: although it is not entirely far-fetched, the beginning of the verse—as mentioned—contradicts it.

It has also been said that the intended meaning is their expulsion and returning them again to where they came from; they were expelled from the Hijaz to the land of Sham and Palestine, having come from them. To this it is replied: the beginning of the verse, in its context, supports something else, as you have known.

Yes, it is possible to say: the intended meaning is the turning of their hearts and the effacement of the faces of their inner selves from the truth toward falsehood, so they do not succeed in faith in Allah and His signs. Furthermore, since the true religion is the path (الصراط) upon which no human succeeds in the happiness of his worldly life except by treading it and remaining upright upon it—and for the one who deviates from it there is nothing but falling into the furnace of corruption and plunging into the depths of destruction—He, the Exalted, said: "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done" (ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا) [30:41]. And He, the Exalted, said: "If the people of the towns had believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied, so We seized them" (وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ ، وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْناهُمْ) [7:96]. A necessary consequence of this reality is that the effacement of faces from true religious knowledge is an effacement of them from the realities of the happiness of worldly life in all its forms. Thus, the one deprived of the happiness of religion is deprived of the happiness of the world—of stability of state, the establishment of security, the nobility of independence and sovereignty, and everything that makes life pleasant and makes the milk of labor flow—except to the extent that religious elements permeate their society. Accordingly, there is no harm in combining most or all of the mentioned views.

His saying, the Exalted: "and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled" (وَكانَ أَمْرُ اللهِ مَفْعُولاً) [4:47]. This is an indication that the command will inevitably occur, and it has occurred according to what Allah mentioned in His Book regarding their being cursed, the descent of displeasure upon them, and the casting of enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, and other things in many verses.

His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes" (إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكا لِمَنْ يَشاءُ) [4:48]. The apparent context is that the verse is in the position of providing the reason for the judgment mentioned in the previous verse—namely His saying: "Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We efface..." etc. Thus, the meaning returns to something like our saying: "For if you do not believe in it, you would thereby be polytheists, and Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, so His wrath and punishment will descend upon you, and He will efface your faces by turning them onto their backs or curse you." Thus, the result of this lack of forgiveness is the arrangement of the worldly effects of polytheism (الشرك) — *the act of ascribing partners to Allah* — such as effacement or cursing upon it.

This is the difference between the content of this verse and His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes. And whoever ascribes partners to Allah has certainly strayed into far-off error" (إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَمَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلالاً بَعِيداً) [4:116]. For this verse (verse 48) threatens with the worldly effects of polytheism, while that one (verse 116) threatens with its otherworldly effects. This is according to the application to the specific case, even if both, in their absolute sense, encompass all effects.

His glorification's forgiveness and lack of forgiveness—neither occurs haphazardly, but rather according to wisdom (الحكمة), and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise. As for His lack of forgiveness for polytheism, creation is only established upon the mercy it contains on the basis of servanthood and Lordship. He, the Exalted, said: "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me" (وَما خَلْفَتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ) [51:56], and there is no servanthood with polytheism. As for His forgiveness for other acts of disobedience and sins that are less than polytheism, it is through the intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — of those for whom He has appointed intercession among the prophets, the saints, the angels, and righteous deeds, as detailed in the discussion on intercession in the first volume of this book.

As for repentance (التوبة), the verse does not address its status from the perspective of the specific case of the verse, because its case is the lack of faith, and there is no repentance without it. Furthermore, with repentance, all sins are forgiven, even polytheism. He, the Exalted, said: "Say, [Allah says,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. And turn penitently to your Lord’" (قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنِيبُوا إِلى رَبِّكُمْ) [39:53-54].

The intended meaning of polytheism in the verse is that which necessarily encompasses faithlessness (الكفر), for the faithless one is also never forgiven, even if the title "polytheist" (المشرك) is not applied to him as a designation, based on the fact that the People of the Book are not called polytheists in the Quran, even if their faithlessness in the Quran and in what the Prophet brought is a polytheism on their part by which they ascribed partners to Him (refer to the commentary on verse 221 of al-Baqarah). When the People of the Book do not believe in what Allah sent down confirming what is with them, they have disbelieved in it and ascribed what is in their hands as partners to Allah the Exalted, for it is something Allah does not desire in the form they have taken it. Thus, the believer in Moses (عليه السلام) who disbelieves in the Messiah (عليه السلام) has disbelieved in Allah and ascribed Moses as a partner to Him. Perhaps what we have mentioned is the subtle point for His saying: "that any partner should be ascribed to Him" (أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ) rather than saying "the polytheist" or "the polytheists."

His saying, the Exalted: "to whomever He wishes" (لِمَنْ يَشاءُ) is a restriction of the speech to repel the illusion that any person has an influence over Him, the Exalted, that would necessitate forgiveness from Him, such that a judge would rule over Him or a subduer would overpower Him. The suspension of established matters in the Quran upon the Will (المشيئة) is frequent, and the reason in all or most of them is to repel the aforementioned illusion, such as His saying, the Exalted: "to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure—except what your Lord may wish—an uninterrupted bounty" (خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ) [11:108].

Furthermore, it is part of wisdom not to forgive every sinner his sin; otherwise, the command and prohibition would be futile, legislation would be nullified, and the matter of divine upbringing would be corrupted. To this, there is an indication in His saying: "to whomever He wishes." From here it appears that for every single act of disobedience, some of its instances must not be forgiven; otherwise, the prohibition against it would be futile. This does not contradict the generality of the language of the verses regarding the causes of forgiveness, for the discourse is about the occurrence, not the promise in an absolute sense. Among acts of disobedience are those that issue from one who is not forgiven due to polytheism and the like.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is that He, the Exalted, does not forgive polytheism from a faithless one or a polytheist, and He forgives other sins besides polytheism through the intercession of an intercessor among His servants or a righteous deed. He, the Exalted, is not compelled to forgive every sin among these sins for every sinner; rather, He may forgive and He may not forgive, all of that for a wisdom.

His saying, the Exalted: "Have you not regarded those who puff themselves up?" (أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ يُزَكُّونَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ) [4:49]. Al-Raghib said: The root of **purification (الزكاة)** is the growth resulting from the blessing of Allah the Exalted—until he said: A person's purification of himself is of two types: one is by action, which is praised, and to this He intended by His saying: "Felicitous is he who purifies himself" (قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّى) [87:14]. The second is by speech, such as his certifying the justice of another; this is blameworthy for a person to do for himself, and Allah the Exalted has forbidden it, saying: "so do not puff yourselves up" (فَلا تُزَكُّوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ) [53:32]. His prohibition of that is an edification because of the ugliness of a person praising himself, both rationally and legally. For this reason, it was said to a wise man: "What is that which is not good even if it is true?" He replied: "A man praising himself." End of his words.

Since the verse is within the verses arranged to address the state of the People of the Book, it appears that these who puff themselves up are the People of the Book or some of them. They were not described as the People of the Book because those who have knowledge of Allah and His signs ought not to clothe themselves in such vices; persistence in them is a stripping away of the Book and its knowledge.

This is supported by what Allah the Exalted narrated from the Jews regarding their saying: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones" (نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللهِ وَأَحِبَّاؤُهُ) [5:18], and their saying: "The Fire shall not touch us except for a few days" (لَنْ تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّاماً مَعْدُودَةً) [2:80], and their claim of guardianship (الولاية) as in His saying, the Exalted: "Say, ‘O Jews! If you claim that you are the allies of Allah, to the exclusion of all other people...’" (قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ هادُوا إِنْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءُ لِلَّهِ مِنْ دُونِ النَّاسِ) [62:6]. Thus, the verse alludes to the Jews, and in it is a testimony to what was previously mentioned in the preceding verses regarding their arrogance against submitting to the truth and following it, and believing in the signs of Allah the Exalted, and the establishment of the divine curse upon them, and that this is among the consequences of their self-admiration and their puffing themselves up.

His saying, the Exalted: "Rather, it is Allah who purifies whomever He wishes, and they shall not be wronged so much as a thread" (بَلِ اللهُ يُزَكِّي مَنْ يَشاءُ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً) [4:49]. This is a turning away from their puffing themselves up, a rejection of what they claimed for themselves, and a clarification that this is among the affairs of Lordship (الربوبية) exclusive to Him, the Exalted. For even if it is possible for a person to be described with virtues and to clothe himself in types of spiritual honor and nobility, his attention to that and reliance upon it is not completed except by his granting himself self-sufficiency and independence, which is in the sense of claiming divinity and partnership with the Lord of the worlds. Where is the poor human—who possesses for himself neither harm nor benefit, nor death nor life—and self-sufficiency from Allah the Exalted in any good or virtue?

The human, in himself and in all his affairs, and the good he claims to possess, and all the causes of that good, are purely the property of Allah the Exalted without exception. So what remains for the human?

This delusion and self-admiration that drives a person to puff himself up is the vanity (العجب) which is among the mothers of vices. Then, this deluded person who relies upon himself soon touches others, and from this vice of his, another vice is born: the vice of arrogance (التكبر). His arrogance is completed in the form of seeking superiority over others among the servants of Allah, whereby he enslaves the servants of Allah the Exalted, and through it, every injustice and transgression without right, the violation of Allah's sanctities, and the extension of authority over people's blood, honor, and wealth are carried out.

All of this is when the description is an individual one. But if it transcends the individual and becomes a social character and a national conduct, it is the danger in which lies the destruction of the species and the corruption of the earth. It is that which He, the Exalted, narrates from the Jews when they said: "We have no obligation toward the gentiles" (لَيْسَ عَلَيْنا فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ سَبِيلٌ) [3:75].

Thus, it is not for a mortal to mention for himself any virtue by which he praises himself, whether he is truthful in what he says or lying, because he does not possess that for himself. However, since Allah the Exalted is the Owner of what He has granted, and the Giver of grace to whomever He wishes and however He wishes, it was for Him to purify whomever He wishes through practical purification by granting grace and bestowing favor, and to purify whomever He wishes through verbal purification by mentioning him with what he is praised for, and honoring him with attributes of perfection. Examples include His saying regarding Adam and Noah: "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah" (إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفى آدَمَ وَنُوحاً) [3:33]; His saying regarding Abraham and Idris: "Indeed he was a truthful one, a prophet" (إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا) [19:41, 56]; His saying regarding Jacob: "and indeed he possessed knowledge because of what We had taught him" (وَإِنَّهُ لَذُو عِلْمٍ لِما عَلَّمْناهُ) [12:68]; His saying regarding Joseph: "Indeed he was one of Our dedicated servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ) [12:24]; His saying regarding Moses: "Indeed he was dedicated, and he was an apostle, a prophet" (إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا) [19:51]; His saying regarding Jesus: "honoured in the world and the Hereafter, and one of those brought near [to Allah]" (وَجِيهاً فِي الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ) [3:45]; His saying regarding Solomon and Job: "What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ) [38:30, 44]; His saying regarding Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Indeed my guardian is Allah, who sent down the Book, and He takes charge of the righteous" (إِنَّ وَلِيِّيَ اللهُ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْكِتابَ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى الصَّالِحِينَ) [7:196]; and His saying: "And indeed you possess a great character" (وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ) [68:4]. Likewise is His saying, the Exalted, regarding a number of prophets He mentioned in Surahs al-An‘am, Maryam, al-Anbiya, al-Saffat, Sad, and others.

In short, purification (التزكية) is a right of Allah the Exalted in which no other shares, for it does not issue from another except from injustice and toward injustice, and it does not issue from Him, the Exalted, except in truth and justice, measured by its proper measure, neither exceeding nor falling short. Therefore, He concluded His saying "Rather, it is Allah who purifies whomever He wishes" with His saying—which is in the sense of providing the reason—: "and they shall not be wronged so much as a thread" (وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً).

It has become clear from what has passed that His purification, although absolute and encompassing practical and verbal purification, applies according to the context of the speech to verbal purification.

His saying, the Exalted: "and they shall not be wronged so much as a thread" (وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلاً). **The thread (الفتيل)** is a passive participle from *fatl*, which is twisting. It has been said: the intended meaning is what is in the cleft of the date-stone. It has been said: it is what is in the belly of the date-stone. It has been narrated in reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام): that it is the speck upon the date-stone; the *naqīr* (النقير) is what is on its back, and the *qiṭmīr* (القطمير) is its skin. It has also been said: it is what you twist between your fingers of dirt. In any case, it is an allusion to a contemptible thing that is not reckoned with.

Two matters have become clear through the noble verse: First, it is not for the possessor of virtue to be vain about his virtue and praise himself; rather, it is among the things exclusive to Him, the Exalted. For the apparent meaning of the verse is that Allah is exclusive in purifying everyone who may be clothed in purification. Thus, it is not for one other than the possessor of virtue to purify him except with what Allah has purified him with. This results in the fact that virtues are those which Allah has praised and purified; thus, there is no value for a virtue that the religion does not recognize or name as a virtue. This does not necessitate that the effects of virtues should be nullified among people such that they do not recognize the virtue of the possessor of virtue or honor his status; rather, they are the symbols (شعائر) of Allah and His signs. He, the Exalted, has said: "And whoever venerates the symbols of Allah—indeed that comes from the God-consciousness of hearts" (وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ) [22:32]. Thus, the ignorant one must submit to the scholar and recognize his status, for that is part of following the truth, and He, the Exalted, has said: "Are those who know equal to those who do not know?" (هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَالَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ) [39:9], even if it is not for the scholar to boast of his knowledge and praise himself. The matter regarding all true human virtues is of this state.

Second, what some of our researchers have mentioned—following what was mentioned by Westerners—that "self-reliance" is among human virtues, is a matter the religion does not recognize, nor does it agree with the taste of the Quran. What the Quran sees in that regard is reliance upon Allah and seeking might through Allah. He, the Exalted, said: "Those to whom the people said, ‘All the people have mustered against you, so fear them.’ That only increased them in faith, and they said, ‘Allah is sufficient for us, and He is an excellent trustee’" (الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً وَقالُوا حَسْبُنَا اللهُ وَنِعْمَ الْوَكِيلُ) [3:173]. And He said: "that power belongs entirely to Allah" (أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً) [2:165]. And He said: "Indeed might belongs entirely to Allah" (إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً) [10:65], among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: "Look, how they fabricate lies against Allah..." etc. [4:50]. Their puffing themselves up with the sonship of Allah, His love, His guardianship, and the like is a fabrication against Allah, since Allah did not grant them that. Furthermore, the root of puffing oneself up is a fabrication even if it were based on truth, for it is—as previously explained—the attribution of a partner to Allah, and He has no partner in His kingdom. He, the Exalted, said: "and He has no partner in sovereignty" (وَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ) [17:111].

And His saying: "and that suffices as a manifest sin" (وَكَفى بِهِ إِثْماً مُبِيناً). That is, if there were nothing in puffing oneself up except that it is a fabrication against Allah, it would suffice for it being a manifest sin. The expression with **sin (الإثم)**—which is the blameworthy act that prevents a person from attaining good things and slows them down—is appropriate for this disobedience because it is of the polytheism of polytheism and its branches, preventing the descent of mercy. Likewise, in the polytheism of faithlessness which prevents forgiveness, as occurred in the previous verse: "and whoever ascribes partners to Allah has certainly fabricated a tremendous sin" (وَمَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَقَدِ افْتَرى إِثْماً عَظِيماً) [4:48] after His saying: "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him."

His saying, the Exalted: "Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who believe in Jibt and Ṭāghūt?" (أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْجِبْتِ وَالطَّاغُوتِ) [4:51]. **Jibt and Jibs** refer to everything in which there is no good; it has been said: everything that is worshipped besides Allah the Exalted. **Ṭāghūt (الطاغوت)** is originally a verbal noun like *ṭughyān* (rebellion), used frequently in the sense of the active agent; it has been said: it is every object of worship besides Allah. The verse reveals the occurrence of an event in which some of the People of the Book judged for those who disbelieved against those who believed, saying that the way of the polytheists is better guided than the way of the believers. The believers have nothing but the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — sent down in the Quran confirming what is with them, while the polytheists have nothing but belief in Jibt and Ṭāghūt. Thus, this judgment is an admission from them that the polytheists have a share of the truth, which is the belief in Jibt and Ṭāghūt that Allah the Exalted attributed to them, then Allah cursed them with His saying: "They are the ones whom Allah has cursed" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ اللهُ) [4:52] the verse.

This supports what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (شأن النزول) — *the specific historical circumstances under which a verse was revealed* — that the polytheists of Mecca asked the People of the Book to judge between them and the believers regarding the religion they followed, so they judged for them against the believers. The narration regarding this will come in the following traditional discussion.

The fact that they were possessors of a share of the Book was mentioned so that it would be more effective in the occurrence of censure and blame upon them, for the belief of the scholars of the Book in Jibt and Ṭāghūt, when the Book had clarified their matter to them, is more hideous and atrocious.

His saying, the Exalted: "Do they have a share of sovereignty?" (أَمْ لَهُمْ نَصِيبٌ مِنَ الْمُلْكِ) [4:53] until His saying: "a speck" (نَقِيراً). **The speck (النقير)** is a passive participle from *naqr*, meaning the tiny amount that a bird takes from the ground with a peck of its beak. Another meaning for it was previously mentioned in His saying: "and they shall not be wronged so much as a thread" the verse.

They have mentioned that "or" (أَمْ) in His saying "Do they have a share of sovereignty?" is disjunctive (منقطعة), and the meaning is: "Rather, do they have a share of sovereignty?" The interrogation is one of denial (إنكاري)—meaning: they do not have that.

Some have permitted that "or" be conjunctive (متصلة), saying that the estimation is: "Are they more worthy of prophethood, or do they have a share of sovereignty?" This was rejected on the grounds that the omission of the interrogative *hamzah* is only permissible in the necessity of poetry, and there is no necessity in the Quran. The apparent view is that "or" is conjunctive and that the omitted part is what the previous verse indicates: "Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book," the verse. The estimation is: "Do they have all that they judged with of judgment, or do they have a share of sovereignty, or do they envy the people?" Accordingly, the parts remain consistent and ordered, and the speech remains connected in its course.

The intended meaning of **sovereignty (الملك)** is authority over material and spiritual affairs; thus, it encompasses the sovereignty of prophethood, guardianship, guidance, and the ownership of necks and wealth. This is because it is what is apparent from the context of the preceding and following phrases. For the previous verse alludes to their claim that they possess the power of judgment and ruling over the believers, which is analogous to sovereignty over spiritual virtues. The conclusion of the verse: "then they would not give the people so much as a speck" (فَإِذاً لا يُؤْتُونَ النَّاسَ نَقِيراً) indicates the ownership of material things or what encompasses that. Thus, the intended meaning is broader than the sovereignty of material and spiritual things.

The meaning of the verse thus boils down to something like our saying: "Or do they have a share of the sovereignty that Allah bestowed upon His Prophet through prophethood, guardianship, guidance, and the like? Had they possessed that, they would not have given the people the least of the little that is not reckoned with, due to their stinginess and the evil of their inner selves." Thus, the verse is close in content to His saying, the Exalted: "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, then you would surely withhold them for fear of spending’" (قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذاً لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفاقِ) [17:100].

His saying, the Exalted: "Or do they envy the people for what Allah has given them out of His grace?" (أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ) [4:54]. This is the last of the three mentioned parts, and the speech is directed toward the Jews in response to their judgment against the believers that the religion of the polytheists is better guided than theirs.

The intended meaning of "the people" (الناس), according to what this context indicates, is those who believe. What Allah has given them of His grace is prophethood, the Book, and religious knowledge. However, the conclusion of the verse: "We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham..." etc. indicates that those who were referred to as "the people" are from the progeny of Abraham. Thus, the intended meaning of "the people" at that point is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and whatever of this mentioned grace was spread to others besides him is through his way and his lofty blessings. It was previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah and the progeny of Abraham..." the verse [3:33], that the progeny of Abraham are the Prophet and his progeny.

The application of "the people" to a singular individual is not problematic, for it is by way of allusion, like your saying to someone who confronts and harms you: "Do not confront the people," and "What have you to do with the people?" intending yourself—meaning: do not confront me.

His saying, the Exalted: "We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom" (فَقَدْ آتَيْنا آلَ إِبْراهِيمَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ) [4:54]. The phrase is to make them despair in their envy and to cut off their hope for the cessation of this blessing and the termination of this grace, by stating that Allah has already given the progeny of Abraham of His grace what He has given, and bestowed upon them of His mercy what He has bestowed. So let them die in their rage, for envy shall not benefit them at all.

From here it appears that the intended meaning of the progeny of Abraham is either the Prophet and his progeny from the descendants of Ishmael, or the progeny of Abraham in general from the descendants of Ishmael and Isaac, so that it encompasses the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who is the one truly envied by the Jews. The intended meaning of the progeny of Abraham is not the Children of Israel from the lineage of Abraham, for the speech on this estimation would become a confirmation for the Jews in their envy of the Prophet or the believers because of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) presence among them; thus, the meaning of the phrase would be corrupted, as is not hidden.

It has also appeared, as previously alluded to, that this phrase: "We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham..." etc. indicates that the envied people are from the progeny of Abraham. This supports the view that the intended meaning of "the people" is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). As for those who believe in him, they are not all from the progeny of Abraham, and there is no special honor for his progeny among the believers over others such that the speech should be carried to them. Mere faith and following the creed of Abraham does not necessitate naming the followers as the "progeny of Abraham." Similarly, His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed the nearest of people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this Prophet and those who believe" (إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْراهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) [3:68] does not necessitate naming those who believe as the "progeny of Abraham" because of the nearness, for in the verse there is a mention of those who followed Abraham, and they are certainly not called the progeny of Abraham. Thus, the intended meaning of the progeny of Abraham is the Prophet, or he and his progeny (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and Ishmael his forefather and those in his line.

His saying, the Exalted: "and We have given them a great kingdom" (وَآتَيْناهُمْ مُلْكاً عَظِيماً) [4:54]. It was previously mentioned that the requirement of the context is that the intended meaning of "kingdom" is what encompasses the spiritual kingdom, of which prophethood and true guardianship over the guidance and instruction of people are a part. This is supported by the fact that Allah the Exalted does not deem worldly kingdom as "great" if it does not lead to a spiritual virtue and a religious merit. This is also supported by the fact that Allah the Exalted did not count prophethood and guardianship among what He counted of grace regarding the progeny of Abraham when He said: "We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book and wisdom." Thus, it is strengthened that prophethood and guardianship are included in the absolute sense of His saying: "and We have given them a great kingdom."

His saying, the Exalted: "Some of them believed in him, and some of them barred [others] from him" (فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ آمَنَ بِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ صَدَّ عَنْهُ) [4:55]. **Barring (الصد)** is turning away. Faith was contrasted with barring because the Jews were not content with merely not believing in what was sent down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without exerting the utmost of their effort in barring people from the way of Allah and from believing in what He sent down of the Book. Perhaps "barring" was in the sense of turning away (الإعراض), in which case the contrast is completed without additional consideration.

His saying, the Exalted: "and hell suffices as a blaze" (وَكَفى بِجَهَنَّمَ سَعِيراً) [4:55]. This is a threat to them with the blaze of Hell in return for their barring from faith in the Book and their kindling the fire of temptation against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and those who believed with him.

Then He, the Exalted, clarified the sufficiency of Hell in their matter with His saying: "Indeed those who disbelieve in Our signs..." [4:56] to the end of the verse, which is a clarification in the form of providing the reason. Then He followed it with His saying: "But those who believe and do righteous deeds..." [4:57] to the end of the verse, so that the difference between the two groups—"those who believed in him" and "those who barred from him"—would be clear, and it would appear that they are at two opposite poles of the happiness and wretchedness of the next life: entering gardens and their deep shade, and being encompassed by the blaze of Hell and burning in the Fire—may Allah protect us. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their right owners, and when you judge..." etc. [4:58]. The second segment of the verse: "and when you judge between people, to judge with justice" (وَإِذا حَكَمْتُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ أَنْ تَحْكُمُوا بِالْعَدْلِ) is apparently linked to the verses preceding it. For the divine clarification therein revolves around the judgment of the Jews for the polytheists that they are better guided in their way than the believers. Allah the Exalted described them at the beginning of His clarification as having been given a share of the Book, and what is in the Book is the clarification of the signs of Allah and divine knowledge. These are trusts (أمانات) for which a covenant has been taken that they be clarified to the people and not concealed from their rightful people.

These mentioned indications support the view that the intended meaning of "trusts" is what encompasses financial trusts and others among spiritual ones, such as true sciences and knowledge, which their bearers have a duty to convey to their rightful people among mankind.

In short, when the Jews betrayed the divine trusts deposited with them—consisting of the knowledge of the doctrines of monotheism and the signs of the prophethood of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and concealed them and did not manifest them at their obligatory time, then were not content with that until they acted unjustly in the judgment between the believers and the polytheists, judging for idolatry over monotheism, their matter therein led to the divine curse and that dragged them to the punishment of the Blaze. When their affair was as it was, He, the Glorified, changed the context of the speech from the first person to the third person and commanded the people to deliver the trusts to their owners and to be just in judgment, saying: "Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their right owners, and when you judge between people..." etc.

That which we have used to broaden the meaning of "rendering trusts" and "justice in judgment" is what the context dictates, as you have come to know. Thus, it cannot be objected that this is a departure from the apparent meaning (الظاهر) of the terms "trust" (الأمانة) and "judgment" (الحكم). For what is immediately understood at the level of legislation (التشريع) from the content of the verse is the obligation to return financial trusts to their owners, and the justice of the judge—which is the ruling in the context of legal adjudication (القضاء الشرعي). This is because absolute legislation is not restricted by the limitations that bind the subjects of subsidiary rulings (الأحكام الفرعية) in jurisprudence (الفقه). Rather, the Quran, for instance, clarifies the obligation of returning trusts in an absolute sense, and the obligation of justice in judgment in an absolute sense. Whatever pertains to jurisprudence—such as financial trusts and adjudication in litigation—is referred to jurisprudence; and whatever is beyond that, the science of the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) derives benefit from it, and so on.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Rifa‘ah ibn Zayd ibn al-Tabut was among the notables of the Jews. Whenever he spoke to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he would twist his tongue and say: 'Give us a hearing (أرعنا سمعك), O Muhammad, so that we may make you understand.' Then he would malign Islam and disparage it. So Allah revealed concerning him: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ يَشْتَرُونَ الضَّلالَةَ** (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book, who buy error...) [4:44] up to His saying: **فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً** (...so they do not believe, except a few) [4:46]."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi regarding the saying of the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ** (O you who were given the Book...) [4:47]—he said: "It was revealed concerning Malik ibn al-Sayf and Rifa‘ah ibn Zayd ibn al-Tabut from the Banu Qaynuqa‘."

In the same source, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله spoke to the leaders of the Jewish rabbis—among them Abdullah ibn Suriya and Ka‘b ibn Asad—and said to them: 'O assembly of Jews, be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and submit (أسلموا), for by Allah, you surely know that what I have brought to you is the truth.' They replied: 'We do not recognize that, O Muhammad.' So Allah revealed concerning them: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ آمِنُوا بِما نَزَّلْنا** (O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down...) [4:47]."

I say: The apparent meaning (ظاهر) of the noble verses, as per the previous commentary (البيان), indicates that although they were revealed concerning the Jews from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), what we have cited regarding the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول) is nothing more than an instance of application (حكم تطبيقي), like most similar reports narrating the occasions of revelation. And Allah knows best.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Nu‘mani by his chain of narrators from Jabir, from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام in a long tradition describing the emergence of the Sufyani, in which he said: "The commander of the Sufyani's army will descend upon al-Bayda’, and a caller will cry out from the heavens: 'O Bayda’, destroy the people!' Then the earth will swallow them—none shall escape except three individuals—Allah will turn their faces toward their backs (أقفيتهم), and they are from the tribe of Kalb. Concerning them, this verse was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ آمِنُوا بِما نَزَّلْنا مُصَدِّقاً لِما مَعَكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَطْمِسَ وُجُوهاً فَنَرُدَّها عَلى أَدْبارِها** (O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards...) [4:47]." I say: It was also narrated by al-Mufid by his chain from Jabir from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام in a similar report regarding the story of the Sufyani.

In *al-Faqih*, by his chain from Thuwayr from his father: That Ali عليه‌السلام said: "There is no verse in the Quran more beloved to me than the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes) [4:48]." I say: It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Faryabi and al-Tirmidhi, who graded it as fair (حسنه), from Ali.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Umar, who said: "When the verse **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ** (O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls...) [39:53] was revealed, a man stood up and said: 'And polytheism (الشرك), O Prophet of Allah?' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله disliked that, so the verse was revealed: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him...) [4:48]."

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Abu Mijlaz, who said: "When this verse was revealed: **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا** (O My servants who have committed excesses...) [39:53], the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله stood upon the pulpit and recited it to the people. A man stood up and asked: 'And polytheism (الشرك) with Allah?' He remained silent—twice or thrice—then this verse was revealed: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes) [4:48]. Thus, the former was established in *al-Zumar* and the latter in *al-Nisa’*."

I say: You have already learned that the verse in *al-Zumar*, based on the verses that follow it, clearly refers to forgiveness through repentance (التوبة). There is no doubt that through repentance, every sin is forgiven, even polytheism (الشرك). However, the verse in *al-Nisa’* pertains to a context other than repentance. Therefore, there is no contradiction between the content of the two verses such that one would be an abrogator (ناسخ) or a specifier (مخصص) for the other.

In *al-Majma‘*, from al-Kalbi: Regarding the verse: "It was revealed concerning the polytheists, Wahshi and his companions. This was because when he killed Hamzah—having been promised manumission for the act—the promise was not fulfilled. When he arrived in Mecca, he and his companions regretted their deed. They wrote to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'We regret what we have done, and nothing prevents us from Islam except that we heard you say while you were in Mecca: **وَالَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ وَلا يَقْتُلُونَ النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلا يَزْنُونَ** (Those who do not invoke another god besides Allah, and do not kill the soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause, and do not commit adultery...) [25:68-69]. We have indeed invoked another god with Allah, killed the soul Allah made inviolable, and committed adultery. Were it not for this, we would have followed you.' Then the verse was revealed: **إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلاً صالِحاً** (...excepting those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously) [25:70]. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent these verses to Wahshi and his companions. When they read them, they wrote back: 'This is a difficult condition—we fear we may not perform righteous deeds and thus not be among the people of this verse.' Then the verse was revealed: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ** (Indeed Allah does not forgive...) [4:48]. He sent it to them, and they read it, then sent back: 'We fear we may not be among the people of His will (مشيئته).' Then the verse was revealed: **يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً** (O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls! Do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins) [39:53]. He sent it to them, and when they read it, he and his companions entered Islam and returned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who accepted them. Then he said to Wahshi: 'Tell me how you killed Hamzah.' When he told him, he said: 'Woe to you! Hide your person from me.' Thereafter, Wahshi went to Syria and remained there until he died."

I say: Al-Razi mentioned this narration in his *Tafsir* from Ibn Abbas. Contemplation (التأمل) upon the contexts of these verses—which the narration claims the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was using to correspond with Wahshi—leaves no doubt for the observer that the narration is fabricated (موضوعة). Its fabricator intended to suggest that Wahshi and his companions were forgiven regardless of whatever major or minor sins they committed. He picked various verses from different places in the Quran—the exception from one place and the excepted from another—despite each being situated in a context surrounded by related parts, forming a cohesive flow (سياق) that does not admit such fragmentation and separation. He cut them, then arranged and ordered them in a manner suited to this strange correspondence between the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and Wahshi.

Some commentators did well when they remarked after pointing to this narration: "It is as if they are trying to prove that Allah, the Glorified, was jesting with Wahshi."

The fabricator of the narration only intended to honor Wahshi with a guaranteed, sealed forgiveness that would not be harmed by any sin he committed or any atrocity he perpetrated. This would imply the removal of retribution for sins, which necessitates the lifting of religious obligations (التكاليف) from mankind, as held by the Christians—or rather, even more repugnant. For they only lifted obligations through a ransom like Jesus the Messiah, whereas this lifts them to follow the whims of Wahshi.

This Wahshi was a slave of Ibn Mut‘im who killed Hamzah at Uhud, then fled to Mecca, and later embraced Islam after the conquest of Ta’if. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: "Hide your person from me." He then went to Syria and settled in Homs. During the era of Umar, he worked as a scribe in the administrative bureau (الديوان), but was later removed for being a habitual wine-drinker, for which he was flogged more than once. He died during the caliphate of Uthman; it is narrated that wine killed him.

Ibn Abd al-Barr narrated in *al-Isti‘ab* by his chain from Ibn Ishaq, from Abdullah ibn al-Fadl, from Sulayman ibn Yasar, from Ja‘far ibn Amr ibn Umayyah al-Damri, who said: "I and Abdullah ibn Adi ibn al-Khiyar set out and passed through Homs, where Wahshi was. We said: 'What if we go to him and ask him about his killing of Hamzah, how he killed him?' We met a man while asking about him, and he said: 'He is a man overcome by wine. If you find him sober, you will find him an Arab man who will tell you whatever you wish of history; but if you find him otherwise, then turn away from him.'" He said: "So we proceeded until we reached him..." the tradition continues, mentioning the manner of his killing Hamzah on the day of Uhud.

In *al-Majma‘*, Mutarrif ibn Shikhkhir narrated from Umar ibn al-Khattab, who said: "During the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, if a man among us died while persisting in a major sin (كبيرة), we would testify that he was of the people of the Fire—until this verse was revealed, then we refrained from such testimonies."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated via Mu‘tamir ibn Sulayman from Sulayman ibn Utbah al-Bariqi, who said: "Isma‘il ibn Thawban narrated to us: 'I was present in the mosque before the Great Plague, and I heard them saying: **مَنْ قَتَلَ مُؤْمِناً** (Whoever kills a believer...) [4:93] to the end of the verse. The Muhajirun and Ansar said: "The Fire has become mandatory for him." But when **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** (Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner should be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes) [4:48] was revealed, they said: "As Allah wills; Allah does what He wishes."'"

I say: Something similar to these two narrations has been reported from Ibn Umar through several chains. These reports are not without flaws. We do not think that the generality of the companions of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would be ignorant of the fact that this verse—**إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ**—does not add anything in its content to the verses of intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — as previously explained. Nor would they be heedless of the fact that most verses of intercession are Meccan, such as the saying of the Exalted in Surah al-Zukhruf: **وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** (Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]) [43:86]. Similar are the verses of intercession in Surahs Yunus, al-Anbiya’, Taha, Saba’, al-Najm, and al-Muddaththir; all are Meccan verses establishing intercession as previously explained. They are general for all sins, restricted on the side of the one interceded for by the pleasing religion—which is monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the negation of partners, and on the side of Allah the Exalted by His will (المشيئة). Thus, the sum of their meaning is the inclusion of forgiveness for all sins except polytheism (الشرك), based on a will from Allah. This is exactly the meaning of this verse: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ**.

As for the verses that threaten the killer of an inviolable soul without right, the consumer of usury (الربا), and the one who severs kinship ties with the punishment of eternal Fire—such as the saying of the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَقْتُلْ مُؤْمِناً مُتَعَمِّداً فَجَزاؤُهُ جَهَنَّمُ خالِداً فِيها** (And whoever kills a believer intentionally, his punishment shall be hell, to remain in it forever...) [4:93], and His saying regarding usury: **وَمَنْ عادَ فَأُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ** (...and whoever repeats [the offense]—they shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]) [2:275], and His saying regarding the one who severs kinship: **أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ** (...it is they who shall have the curse, and for them shall be the ills of the [ultimate] abode) [13:25], and other such verses—these verses only threaten with evil and inform of the punishment of the Fire. As for this being a finalized punishment (جزاء محتوماً) that accepts no change or removal, they are not explicit in that regard.

In short, the verse **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ** does not surpass the verses of intercession by any additional matter in its content that would justify what they mentioned.

It was not possible for them to understand from the verses of major sins (الكبائر) a finalized necessity of the Fire such that it would be permissible for them to testify to the Fire for the one who commits them. Nor was it possible for them to understand from the verse of forgiveness (**إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** etc.) something they had not already understood from the verses of intercession, such that it would necessitate the claim of it abrogating, specifying, or qualifying the verses of major sins.

This is hinted at by what appears in some of these narrations, such as what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn al-Durays, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Adi with a sound chain (سند صحيح) from Ibn Umar, who said: "We used to refrain from seeking forgiveness for the people of major sins until we heard from our Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ**, and he said: 'I have reserved my supplication as intercession (شفاعتي) for the people of major sins from my nation.' So we refrained from much of what was in our souls, then we spoke out afterwards and hoped."

The apparent meaning of the narration is that what they understood from the verse of forgiveness, they understood similarly from the hadith of intercession. However, another question remains: why did they understand the permissibility of forgiveness for major sins from the hadith of intercession, yet they did not understand it from the Meccan verses of intercession despite their abundance, clarity, and long-standing presence? I do not know!
