In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ** (Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book...) [4:44] up to His saying: **سَبِيلاً** (...a way) [4:51]: al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* and Ibn Asakir in his *Tarikh* narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah, who said: "When the matter of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reached where it did, Ka‘b ibn al-Ashraf withdrew and went to Mecca. He stayed there and said: 'I will not help against him nor fight him.' It was said to him in Mecca: 'O Ka‘b, is our religion better or the religion of Muhammad and his companions?' He said: 'Your religion is better and older, and the religion of Muhammad is new.' So the verse was revealed concerning him: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا نَصِيباً مِنَ الْكِتابِ**."

I say: There are narrations regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse in various forms, the most sound of which is what we have cited. However, they all share the core of the story: that some of the Jews judged in favor of Quraysh against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, claiming that their religion was better than his.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ** (Do they envy the people for what Allah has given them out of His grace?...) [4:54]: from al-Shaykh in his *Amali*, by his chain from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: "**أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ**—he said: 'We are the people (الناس).'"

In *al-Kafi*, by his chain from Burayd from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام in a tradition: "**أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ**—We are the envied people," the tradition continues.

I say: This meaning is narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام extensively (مستفيضا) through many chains preserved in Shi‘i collections such as *al-Kafi*, *al-Tahdhib*, *al-Ma‘ani*, *al-Basa’ir*, and the *Tafsirs* of al-Qummi and al-Ayyashi, among others.

In the same vein, from Sunni chains, is what is narrated from Ibn al-Maghazli, tracing it back to Muhammad ibn Ali al-Baqir عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of the Exalted: "**أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ عَلى ما آتاهُمُ اللهُ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ**"—he said: "By Allah, we are the people."

And what is in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn al-Mundhir and al-Tabarani via Ata’ from Ibn Abbas: regarding His saying: "**أَمْ يَحْسُدُونَ النَّاسَ**"—he said: "We are the people, to the exclusion of [other] people." It has also been narrated therein that "the people" refers to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, from Ikrimah, Mujahid, Muqatil, and Abu Malik. It has already been mentioned in our previous commentary (البيان) that the apparent meaning is that "the people" refers to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and his Ahl al-Bayt are joined with him.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Humran from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام: "**فَقَدْ آتَيْنا آلَ إِبْراهِيمَ الْكِتابَ** (We have certainly given the progeny of Abraham the Book...) [4:54]—he said: 'Prophethood (النبوة)'; '**وَالْحِكْمَةَ** (...and wisdom)—he said: 'Understanding and judgment (القضاء)'; and '**مُلْكاً عَظِيماً** (...and a great kingdom)—he said: 'Obedience (الطاعة).'"

I say: What is meant by "obedience" is the mandated obedience (الطاعة المفترضة), as mentioned in other traditions. Reports regarding these meanings are also numerous. In some of them, mandated obedience is interpreted as the Imamate (الإمامة) and the Caliphate (الخلافة), as in *al-Kafi* by its chain from Burayd from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا** (Indeed those who deny Our signs...) [4:56]—he said: "The signs (الآيات) are the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams عليهم‌السلام."

I say: This is from the category of application (الجري).

In the *Majalis* of al-Shaykh, by his chain from Hafs ibn Ghiyath the judge, who said: "I was with the master of the Ja‘faris, Ja‘far ibn Muhammad عليه‌السلام, when al-Mansur brought him. Ibn Abi al-Awja’, who was an atheist (ملحد), came to him and asked: 'What do you say about this verse: **كُلَّما نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُمْ بَدَّلْناهُمْ جُلُوداً غَيْرَها لِيَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ** (As often as their skins are consumed, We shall replace them with other skins, so that they may taste the punishment) [4:56]? Suppose these skins disobeyed and were punished, what is the fault of the "other" skins?' Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'Woe to you! It is the same, yet it is other.' He said: 'Make me understand this statement.' He replied: 'Have you not seen that if a man takes a brick and breaks it, then pours water on it and kneads it, then returns it to its original form—is it not the same, yet it is other?' He said: 'Yes, may Allah grant us enjoyment through you.'"

I say: It was also narrated in *al-Ihtijaj* from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from him عليه‌السلام, and by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* without a chain (مرسلاً). The reality of the answer returns to the fact that the unity of matter (المادة) is preserved by the unity of form (الصورة). Thus, the human body, like the parts of his body, remains in its unity as long as the human is the same human, regardless of any change that occurs in the body.

In *al-Faqih*, it is said: Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—**لَهُمْ فِيها أَزْواجٌ مُطَهَّرَةٌ** (...and they will have therein purified spouses) [4:57]. He said: "The purified spouses (الأزواج المطهرة) are those who do not menstruate nor have discharges."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تُؤَدُّوا الْأَماناتِ** (Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts...) [4:58]: from Muhammad ibn Ibrahim al-Nu‘mani by his chain from Zurarah from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn Ali عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَنْ تُؤَدُّوا الْأَماناتِ إِلى أَهْلِها وَإِذا حَكَمْتُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ أَنْ تَحْكُمُوا بِالْعَدْلِ** (Indeed Allah commands you to deliver the trusts to their [rightful] owners, and, when you judge between people, to judge with fairness) [4:58]. He said: 'Allah commanded the Imam to deliver the trust (الأمانة) to the Imam who follows him; he has no right to withhold it from him. Do you not hear His saying: **وَإِذا حَكَمْتُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ أَنْ تَحْكُمُوا بِالْعَدْلِ إِنَّ اللهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُمْ بِهِ** (...and, when you judge between people, to judge with fairness. Excellent indeed is what Allah advises you)? They are the rulers (الحكام), O Zurarah; He addressed the rulers with it.'"

I say: The beginning of the tradition is narrated through many chains from them (peace be upon them), and its conclusion indicates that it is from the category of application (الجري), and that the verse was revealed regarding absolute judgment and giving the rightful person his due. Thus, it applies to instances such as what was mentioned previously.

In the same vein is what is in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Sa‘id ibn Mansur, al-Faryabi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "It is the duty of the Imam to judge by what Allah has revealed and to deliver the trust (الأمانة). If he does that, it is the duty of the people to listen to him, to obey, and to respond when they are called."
